0u18_move_semantics.title: Move Semantics
0u18_move_semantics.content:
Context
Transferring resources from one object to another without deep copying — “pilfering” pointers, buffers, file handles.
Overview
Move constructor: T(T&& other)
Move assignment: T& operator=(T&& other)
The source (other) is left in a valid but unspecified state (usually empty).
Rule of Five
If you define any of: destructor, copy ctor/assign, move ctor/assign — you likely need all five.
Example
class Buffer {
    int* data;
public:
    Buffer(Buffer&& other) noexcept : data(other.data) {
        other.data = nullptr;  // steal, nullify source
    }
};
When Used

Returning by value (if RVO not applied)
Container reallocation (vector moving elements)
std::unique_ptr transfer
std::swap

noexcept Matters
Containers prefer moves that are noexcept. Declare move operations noexcept if possible!
See Also

oqfb_std_move
vf1m_perfect_forwarding
Rule of Five

2q1n_black_swan.title: Black Swan: Obsession and the Price of Perfection
2q1n_black_swan.content:
Black Swan: Obsession and the Price of Perfection
Context

Related: 7hjk_kon_cycle
Prerequisites: None — start here
Next: 7hjk_kon_cycle

Black Swan is a 2010 American psychological horror-thriller film directed by Darren Aronofsky, starring Natalie Portman, Mila Kunis, and Vincent Cassel. The film draws significant inspiration from Satoshi Kon’s Perfect Blue, which Aronofsky has acknowledged.
Nina Sayers, a disciplined ballet dancer in a New York company, wins the lead role in Tchaikovsky’s Swan Lake. To play both the innocent White Swan and the seductive Black Swan, she must tap into her own repressed sexuality and darker impulses. As opening night approaches, Nina’s mental state fractures under pressure, and she begins to hallucinate and harm herself.
Plot Summary
Nina is a technically perfect but emotionally restrained dancer. When artistic director Thomas Leroy casts her as the Swan Queen, he warns her she can only embody the White Swan — the sensual Black Swan must come from within. Her mother’s oppressive control, rivalry with fellow dancer Lily, and Thomas’s psychological manipulation gradually unravel her. Nina experiences doppelgänger visions, body horror, and a growing sense of being hunted by a double, while her performance deteriorates and her physical Health declines.
Themes and Analysis

Perfectionism and self-destruction: The film interrogates whether artistic excellence requires sacrificing one’s sanity and body.
Dual identity: The White Swan/Black Swan dichotomy mirrors Nina’s split between public persona and private desires.
Gaze and objectification: Frequent mirror imagery, voyeuristic camera angles, and sexualized framing make the audience complicit in Nina’s objectification, just as she is objectified by directors, critics, fans.
Mother-daughter enmeshment: Nina’s controlling mother represents the destructive side of devotion.
Body horror: Transformations, bleeding toes, skin rips, and the grotesque final makeup visually encode psychological collapse.

Connection to Perfect Blue
Aronofsky has stated the film’s roots are in Perfect Blue, and the parallels are direct:








































Parallel elementPerfect BlueBlack SwanArtistic pursuit requiring transformationIdol → serious actingBallerina → dual roleStalker/double figure”Me-Mania” personalityNina’s Black Swan doppelgängerControlling parent figureNone (age)Nina’s mother EricaReality/unreliable perceptionHallucinations/murdersVisual hallucinations/body horrorClimactic identity collapseMima integrates personalitiesNina fully becomes Black SwanStaircase imageryMima’s apartment stairsNina’s descent into madness
Artistic Significance
Black Swan is a modern classic of psychological horror. Portman’s Oscar-winning performance physically embodies Nina’s deterioration (she lost significant weight and trained for a year in ballet). The film’s cinematography uses fisheye lenses, mirrored spaces, and hyper-saturated colors to visually represent psychological decay.
Black Swan is brutal to watch — not just because of its horror imagery, but because it so precisely captures how art can become indistinguishable from self-annihilation. The film is often read as an allegory for the female experience of professional excellence under constant scrutiny, or as a broader metaphor for the artist’s Faustian bargain.

k9p2_perfect_blue - Direct inspiration and thematic predecessor
Suspiria (2018) - Another horror film about dance and transformation
The Red Shoes (1948) - Classic film about artistic obsession destroying a dancer
Requiem for a Dream - Aronofsky’s earlier descent-into-madness film
The Turning Point - Contrasting film about ballet without horror

3t4g_magnetic_rose.title: Magnetic Rose: Haunting Memories of Love and Loss
3t4g_magnetic_rose.content:
Magnetic Rose: Haunting Memories of Love and Loss
Context

Related: 7hjk_kon_cycle
Prerequisites: None — start here
Next: 7hjk_kon_cycle

Magnetic Rose is a 1995 science fiction horror short film, the first of three shorts comprising Katsuhiro Otomo’s anthology film Memories (based on stories by Otomo). Directed by Koji Morimoto and written by Satoshi Kon (in his screenwriting debut), it stands as a precursor to Perfect Blue.
In the year 2095, a deep-space salvage crew answers a distress call from a derelict spaceship adrift near Saturn. They discover a grand opera house orbiting the wreck, with a lone woman’s ghost — Eva — who appears in holographic form and plays recordings of opera singer her deceased lover. The crew members become drawn into her memories and illusions, which merge past trauma and present danger.
Plot Summary
Salvagers Heintz and Miguel respond to a distress signal from an abandoned vessel. They find a magnificent but abandoned spaceship whose interior has been transformed into a reproduction of a 19th-century opera house. They encounter Eva, a beautiful woman who claims to have been left alone when her lover, the operatic soprano Carlo, died. As they attempt to repair her ship, her holographic illusions begin to manipulate their perceptions, playing on their own repressed memories and loves. Miguel becomes entranced by her performance, and Heintz must confront his own past to escape.
Themes and Analysis

Memory as haunting: Eva’s trauma has physically manifested in her ship’s systems, creating literal ghosts.
Love and obsession: Eva’s love for Carlo never dies; it weaponizes itself against anyone who enters.
Grief as programming: The ship’s automated systems continue to replay Eva’s tragedy endlessly.
Perception and reality: The crew cannot trust their senses, as Eva manipulates light and sound.
Historical layering: The film explores how the past persists technologically — a ghost in the machine.

Connection to Perfect Blue
Satoshi Kon wrote this screenplay 2 years before directing Perfect Blue, and you can see his emerging auteur concerns:

Female vanishing/phantom: Both films feature a central female figure who is less a person than a projection of memory/desire.
Male perception of women: Both critiquing how male characters view (and are destroyed by) the female characters.
Haunting as psychological: The ghosts are not external; they are manifestations of the survivors’ own psyches.
Surreal staging: Both use surreal, stage-like spatial design to represent psychological interiors.

Artistic Significance
Memories as a whole is significant for uniting three visionary directors (Otomo, Morimoto, and Kon’s early script). Magnetic Rose set the visual tone for what would become Kon’s career — meticulous realism distorted by subjective psychological intrusion. It’s one of the few horror films where the horror is not in gore but in the beauty itself becoming predatory.
Comparison with other Memories segments
Memories contains three shorts:

Magnetic Rose (Kon/Morimoto) — psychological space horror
Stink Bomb (otomo) — black comedy body horror
Cannon Fodder (otomo) — anti-war steampunk allegory

At only 30 minutes, Magnetic Rose is a perfect short — every image serves multiple functions. Its tragedy is profound: Eva is simultaneously a victim (of lost love) and a predator (destroying new life to sustain her memory). The opera soundtrack (Puccini’s Madama Butterfly) reinforces the doomed romance. This short proves that Kon was refining the language of Perfect Blue long before his feature debut.

k9p2_perfect_blue - Kon’s later feature film sharing core themes
p6m7_paprika - Kon’s final film also about projected consciousness
8xhr_paranoia_agent - Kon’s series expanding trauma/phantoms concept
Ghost in the Shell (1995 film) - Contemporary cyberpunk examining memory/identity
Ring (film) - Japanese horror film featuring a phantom haunting recording
Memories (1995 film) - The anthology containing Magnetic Rose

76kn_Systemd-User-Services.title: Systemd User Services & %t Runtime Variable
76kn_Systemd-User-Services.content:
Systemd User Services & the %t Runtime Variable
Context

Related: ?
Prerequisites: Basic systemd knowledge
See also: systemctl --user docs, XDG Base Directory spec

Overview
User-level systemd services allow non-root users to manage their own background daemons without sudo. Services are defined in ~/.config/systemd/user/ and controlled via systemctl --user.
The %t specifier is a context-aware runtime directory variable:

Root-level service → %t = /run (global system runtime dir)
User-level service → %t = $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR (e.g., /run/user/1000)

This enables secure socket-based IPC (e.g., ssh-agent) isolated per user.

Quick Reference
Configuration Locations




















LevelPathManaged WithSystem-wide/etc/systemd/system/sudo systemctlUser-level~/.config/systemd/user/systemctl --user
Essential Commands
systemctl --user daemon-reload
systemctl --user start <service>
systemctl --user stop <service>
systemctl --user enable --now <service>
systemctl --user status <service>
systemctl --user list-units --type=service
Service File Template
~/.config/systemd/user/myapp.service:
[Unit]
Description=My Background Service
After=network.target
 
[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/path/to/program
Restart=always
RestartSec=5
 
[Install]
WantedBy=default.target

The %t Specifier
Resolution Table




















Context%t ValueExampleSystem service (root)/run%t/my.socket → /run/my.socketUser service$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR%t/ssh-agent.socket → /run/user/1000/ssh-agent.socket
Why This Matters

/run is root:root — users cannot write there
$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR is owned by the user — isolated, no sudo
Same file works at both levels; %t adapts automatically


Key Benefits

No Sudo Required — users manage their own daemons
Per-User Isolation — each user gets /run/user/$UID
Auto Cleanup — tmpfs, cleared on logout/reboot
XDG Compliant — follows base directory spec


Common Use Cases

ssh-agent socket
GPG agent
Syncthing (file sync)
Rootless Docker / Podman
Local dev servers (HTTP, DB)
Mail indexing (mbsync, notmuch)


Troubleshooting
”Failed to connect to bus”
The user daemon isn’t running:
systemctl --user daemon-reload
sudo loginctl enable-linger $USER
systemctl --user exit && systemctl --user
Check runtime dir
echo $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR
ls -la $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR

Related

systemd --user — per-user systemd instance
XDG_RUNTIME_DIR — user runtime base
loginctl enable-linger — persist after logout
Rootless containers

7hjk_kon_cycle.title: Satoshi Kon & Psychological Horror: Connected Works
7hjk_kon_cycle.content:
Satoshi Kon & Psychological Horror: Connected Works
Context

Related: k9p2_perfect_blue p6m7_paprika 2q1n_black_swan 8xhr_paranoia_agent 3t4g_magnetic_rose
Prerequisites: None — start here
Next: None (standalone)

This map connects the major works exploring psychological reality-collapse, identity fragmentation, and media-criticism horror — primarily from Satoshi Kon’s filmography but also including key related works that share DNA.
Satoshi Kon (1963-2010) crafted a distinctive filmography examining how perception, identity, and reality can fracture under social, technological, or personal pressure. His four feature films and one TV series form a cohesive artistic project examining the constructed self. This note traces those connections and related works outside Kon’s oeuvre that invoke similar concerns.
The Satoshi Kon Cycle
Satoshi Kon’s works form a de facto series even across different stories and genres:








































WorkYearUnique angleMagnetic Rose (segment in Memories)1995Memory as haunted technologyPerfect Blue1997Identity fragmentation under celebrity/mediaMillennium Actress2001Memory/time collapsing as life reviewTokyo Godfathers2003Coincidence/fate vs. ordinary realism (less psychological)Paprika2006Collective dream invasion, reality mergingParanoia Agent2004 (TV)Societal-scale paranoia and urban legend
Evolutionary arc: Kon moved from individual psychological horror (Perfect Blue) → collective dreams (Paprika) → social-scale breakdown (Paranoia Agent) → personal memory/time (Millennium Actress) — but all are concerned with the unreliability of subjective experience.
Beyond Kon: Related Films
Films directly influenced by Kon

Black Swan (Aronofsky, 2010) — Adaptation of Perfect Blue’s obsession/identity themes into ballet context
Inception (Nolan, 2010) — Dream-within-dream structure owes much to Paprika (released only 4 years prior)

Parallel directors exploring similar territory

David Lynch (Mulholland Drive, Lost Highway) — Reality collage, doppelgängers, Hollywood critique
David Cronenberg (Videodrome, Rosemary’s Baby, Naked Lunch) — Technology/physical/psychological fusion body-horror
Alejandro González Iñárritu (Birdman, The Revenant) — Reality/time collapse, identity fragmentation

Shared Motifs Across These Works








































MotifAppears as…ExamplesDoppelgängerSplit identity, stalking doubleMima’s stalker, Nina’s Black Swan, Eva’s projectionMirror/ReflectionSelf-watching, objectificationPerfect Blue mirrors; Black Swan studio mirrorsPerformance pressureCodified perfection destroying selfIdol → actress; Ballerina → Swan; Actor dissociatingMedia mediates realityNews/TV as psychological intrusionParanoia Agent’s press; Perfect Blue’s paparazziStaircase imageryDescent into psycheMima’s stairs; Nina’s spiral staircaseGrandiose finaleVisual/psychic collapse → transcendenceParade → Paprika’s final restorative wave; Black Swan’s stage death
These notes represent a coherent body of horror that is psychological first and supernatural second. The monsters are internal. The ghosts are our own perceptions turning against us. The real threat is the way we are seen by others and how we internalize that gaze. Kon’s genius was showing how technology (media, recording, dreams, machines) can mediate and magnify those pressures into horror.
If you’re exploring this body of work, watch them in roughly this order:

Magnetic Rose (short) — Kon’s thesis statement
Perfect Blue — Masterwork of individual breakdown
Paranoia Agent — Distillation to social sphere
Paprika — synthesis into transcendent possibility
Black Swan & Inception — Forking paths in Western cinema


k9p2_perfect_blue - The anchor film of the cycle
p6m7_paprika - The dream culmination
2q1n_black_swan - The Western adaptation
8xhr_paranoia_agent - The social expansion
3t4g_magnetic_rose - The manifesto in embryo
Millennium Actress - The memory/time meditation
Requiem for a Dream - Another descent-into-obsession film (Aronofsky)

84ey_tmp.title: Template Metaprogramming (TMP)

8xhr_bleood.title: bleood
8xhr_bleood.content:
bleood
Context

Related: ?

Overview
bleood is a rapper from New York City. Active since 2005, producing experimental and underground hip hop, with strong rage and digicore influences.
Main Content
Identity & Origin

Name: bleood (stylized lowercase)
Origin: New York City, USA
Active Since: March 17, 2005
MusicBrainz ID: ce6a32ee-197d-4a59-9dad-37eb21b45c30

Musical Style & Genres

Experimental hip hop
Underground hip hop
Rage
Digicore


Indicates an internet-native, production-forward sound likely with distorted 808s and aggressive delivery.

Discography



































YearTitleFormat2025painAlbum (Mixtape/Street)2023Seal of MemoriesAlbum2025rascal 51Album (?)2023chapter 2 bEP2022takemebackEP
Notable Tracks
NOGWAPNOLIFE (2025)
NOGWAPNOLIFE appears on the rascal 51 album (released October 2025 via DistroKid). The track stands out for its distinctive vocal chops — particularly the sampled line “I am sorry, did I hurt you” which slices through the mix with emotional impact.
Production style:

Short runtime: ~2:25 (typical rage/digicore punchiness)
Aggressive, compressed drums with distorted 808s
Chopped vocal sample processed through heavy effects (likely pitched/reversed)
Minimalist arrangement that lets the vocal hit hard
Internet-native sound design — feels optimized for short-form platforms (TikTok, YouTube Shorts)

The vocal sample acts as both a melodic hook and rhythmic device, delivering the track’s emotional core in just a few words. Production is clean, forward, and maximal in its simplicity — no wasted elements.

This track exemplifies the rage/digicore aesthetic: short, explosive, emotionally raw, and built for repeat streaming.


Career Pattern

~2005–2019: Early underground period (pre-digicore era?)
2022–2025: concentrated output in rage/digicore style
Independent artist with no major label affiliations visible

Remarks

Confirmed via MusicBrainz API (no label / website relations captured)
No official site or streaming platform artist profile linked in data
Niche genre tags suggest small-but-dedicated following in underground circuits
Case-sensitive artist name: lowercase “bleood”

8xhr_paranoia_agent.title: Paranoia Agent: Society's Collective Nervous Breakdown
8xhr_paranoia_agent.content:
Paranoia Agent: Society’s Collective Nervous Breakdown
Context

Related: 7hjk_kon_cycle
Prerequisites: None — start here
Next: 7hjk_kon_cycle

Paranoia Agent is a 2004 Japanese psychological thriller anime television series created and directed by Satoshi Kon, who wrote and directed every episode. It was his only TV series and expands on themes from Perfect Blue and Paprika into a social context.
The series is set in a Tokyo suffocating under social pressure and economic insecurity. After a mysterious assailant known as “Lil’ Slugger” (or “Shōnen Bat”) begins attacking people with a golden baseball bat, a detective investigation unravels a web of urban legends, mass psychology, and collective delusion. The narrative is non-linear and multi-perspective, exploring how trauma propagates through a society.
Plot Summary
The series centers on Tsukiko Sagi, a stressed character designer who is attacked by Lil’ Slugger after work. The case draws detectives Keiichi Ikari and Mitsuhiro Maniwa into an investigation that crosses paths with various Tokyo residents: a disgraced police officer, a bullied student, a family man cracking under pressure, and internet suicide pacts. As more attacks occur, each victim’s story reveals how the “attacks” function as psychological pressure valves in a repressed society, eventually spiraling into a city-wide mass hallucination.
Episodes Structure
The series is divided into three story arcs, though all are interrelated:

Episodes 1-5: Introduces Tsukiko and the initial investigation, exploring individual cases and the birth of the Lil’ Slugger urban legend.
Episodes 6-10: Expands to secondary characters, their traumas, and how the legend spreads through media and gossip.
Episodes 11-13: The investigation collapses into mass hysteria, as the line between real and imagined attacks fully disappears.

Themes and Analysis

Collective paranoia: How fear can propagate and take on a life of its own, detached from any single perpetrator.
Social pressure and stigma: Each character’s attack is precipitated by a social transgression or pressure point — being bullied, being overworked, being “unusual.”
Urban legend transmission: The series shows how information mutates and gains power as it spreads.
Trauma cycles: Victims often become perpetrators, perpetuating harm.
Media manipulation: The tabloid press and public speculation amplify the panic.
Multiple personality at social scale: Just as Perfect Blue explored individual identity fragmentation, this series asks what happens when an entire society experiences identity fragmentation.

Connection to Perfect Blue & Paprika

Individual → Collective: Perfect Blue explored identity crisis in one person; Paranoia Agent asks what happens when thousands share the same crisis.
Media critique: All three works critique how media shapes and distorts reality.
Visual motifs: Signature Kon fluidity between reality/dream appears here as hallucination versus objective events.
Lil’ Slugger as Paprika’s parade: The swarm of slugs in the finale recalls Paprika’s surreal parade.

Artistic Significance
Paranoia Agent is perhaps the most sophisticated social critique in anime. It accurately anticipated the rise of social media-fueled moral panics, online harassment, and “cancel culture.” Its prediction that collective trauma could reach epidemic proportions feels prescient in the 2020s.
This series is dense, demanding, and bleak. It is not entertaining in a conventional sense; it is a harrowing sociological case study in animated form. The final episode’s surreal transformation of Tokyo into a dormant sea of people is one of television’s most powerful sequences about psychological mass dissociation.

k9p2_perfect_blue - Individual psychological breakdown
p6m7_paprika - Collective dream invasion
Paranoia Agent (manga) - Kon’s own manga adaptation
Serial Experiments Lain - Another series about society/identity/hallucination
Boogiepop Phantom - Similar urban-legend psychological horror anime

dknz_value_categories.title: C++ Value Categories: lvalue, rvalue, xvalue, prvalue, glvalue
dknz_value_categories.content:
Context
Understanding whether an expression is an lvalue, rvalue, or something else is key to mastering references, moves, and overload resolution.
Overview



































CategoryDescriptionExamplelvalueHas identity/memory addressx, *ptr, arr[0]xvalueExpiring value (has identity, can be moved)std::move(x), T&& returnprvaluePure rvalue, no identity (temporary)42, 3.14, T()glvaluelvalue OR xvalue (has identity)rvaluexvalue OR prvalue (can bind to &&)
Simplified: lvalue = named object, rvalue = temporary.
Reference Binding

T& → lvalues only
const T& → any category
T&& → rvalues only (prvalue or xvalue)

int x = 0;
int&  r1 = x;     // OK
int&& r2 = 42;    // OK
int&& r3 = x;     // ERROR (x is lvalue)
int&& r4 = std::move(x); // OK (xvalue)
Takeaway

Move semantics rely on xvalues (objects we can pilfer).
Perfect forwarding preserves the original value category.

See Also

vf1m_perfect_forwarding
oqfb_std_move
hi6f_universal_refs

fr7z_Essential-Chinese-Characters.title: Essential Chinese Characters for Beginners
fr7z_Essential-Chinese-Characters.content:
Essential Chinese Characters for Beginners
Context

Related: ?
Prerequisites: None — start here
Next: HSK Level 1 Vocabulary

Overview
A curated list of high-frequency simplified Chinese characters (汉字, hànzì) for absolute beginners. Focus on reading recognition first — these characters appear everywhere in daily life, signs, menus, and basic text.
Each entry includes: character, pinyin, English meaning, and a simple usage example.
Core Characters (60 essential starters)
Pronouns & People









































CharPinyinMeaningExample我wǒI / me我叫小明 — My name is Xiaoming你nǐyou (singular)你好 — Hello他tāhe / him他是学生 — He is a student她tāshe / her她是老师 — She is a teacher人rénperson / people中国人 — Chinese person
Common Verbs



























































CharPinyinMeaningExample是shìto be / is我是学生 — I am a student有yǒuto have我有书 — I have a book去qùto go我们去学校 — We go to school来láito come请进 — Please come in吃chīto eat吃饭 — Eat rice / have a meal喝hēto drink喝水 — Drink water说shuōto speak说中文 — Speak Chinese看kànto see / look看电视 — Watch TV
Common Nouns











































































CharPinyinMeaning日rìsun / day月yuèmoon / month年niányear国guócountry中zhōngmiddle / China大dàbig / large小xiǎosmall多duōmany / much少shǎofew / little水shuǐwater火huǒfire山shānmountain木mùtree / wood
Numbers 1–10




























































CharPinyinValue一yī1二èr2三sān3四sì4五wǔ5六liù6七qī7八bā8九jiǔ9十shí10
Grammar Particles



































CharPinyinRole个gègeneral measure word的depossessive particle了lepast tense / completion不bùno / not很hěnvery
Time & Place













































CharPinyinMeaning天tiānsky / day早zǎoearly / morning晚wǎnevening / late上shàngup / on下xiàdown / under前qiánfront / before后hòuback / after
Stroke Order Basics

Top → Bottom: 人 — first stroke top, second bottom
Left → Right: 大 — left horizontal, then right cross
Horizontal → Vertical: 十 — horizontal first
Enclosure → Inside: 口 — draw box, then midpoint

Learning Tips
Radicals (部首, bùshǒu) hint at meaning:

氵 (three drops water) → water: 水, 河, 海
木 (tree) → wood/plant: 林, 森, 树
火 (fire) → fire/heat: 炎, 点, 热
⼈ (person) → person: 他, 你, 从

Common components:

女 (nǚ, woman) → 她, 好, 妈
子 (zǐ, child) → 学, 字, 孩
言 (yán, speech) → 说, 话, 语

Study Strategy

Daily: 10–15 new characters + review (use flashcards)
Write each 5–10 times — stroke order matters!
Group by meaning — not alphabetically (people → food → places)
Contextualize: Note the radical and common words it appears in

Resources

Apps: Pleco (dictionary), Skritter (writing), Du Chinese (readers)
Websites: Zhongwen (pop-up dict), Arch Chinese (stroke order), YellowBridge (radicals)
YouTube: Yoyo Chinese, ChinesePod, Everyday Chinese

h9ft_ts_cpp_templates.title: TypeScript Generics vs C++ Templates
h9ft_ts_cpp_templates.content:
Context
If you know TypeScript generics, C++ templates may feel familiar but have critical differences in when and how they operate.
Overview








































AspectTypeScript GenericsC++ TemplatesWhenCompile-time type erasure; single JS outputCompile-time code generation; unique code per instantiationPurposeType hints only; no runtime impactGenerates actual machine code for each typeSpecializationNo (except function overloads)Yes — full partial/explicit specializationMetaprogrammingLimited (keyof, typeof, conditional types)Turing-complete (constexpr if, SFINAE, recursion, concepts)Error locationPoint of use (after erasure)Point of definition (instantiation)CompilationFast (single pass)Slow (header bloat, repeated parsing)
TypeScript Generics (Erasure)
function identity<T>(arg: T): T { return arg; }
const n = identity<number>(42);  // same JS code for any T

Type parameters exist only at compile time.
No code generation per type; single JavaScript emitted.
Type constraints: T extends SomeInterface

C++ Templates (Code Generation)
template<typename T>
T identity(T arg) { return arg; }
int n = identity(42);   // instantiates `int identity<int>(int)`
double d = identity(3.14); // instantiates `double identity<double>(double)`

Each instantiation is a separate function/class.
Type parameters are real types — can use sizeof(T), is_pointer_v<T>.

Key Takeaway

TS generics = type-level placeholders, erased at runtime.
C++ templates = compile-time code generation, zero-cost abstraction.

See Also

vf1m_perfect_forwarding
dknz_value_categories

hi6f_universal_refs.title: Universal and Forwarding References
hi6f_universal_refs.content:
Context
A T&& parameter where T is a deduced template parameter can bind to both lvalues and rvalues. This is a forwarding reference (aka universal reference).
Overview
template<typename T>
void f(T&& arg);  // forwarding reference — T is deduced
 
void g(MyType&& arg);  // rvalue reference — MyType is fixed, not deduced

f can accept both lvalues and rvalues.
Deduction: lvalue → T = X& → parameter becomes X&; rvalue → T = X → parameter becomes X&&.

Reference Collapsing
Rules make T& && collapse to T&, enabling lvalue binding.
Perfect Forwarding
Use std::forward<T>(arg) inside to preserve the original value category.
See Also

vf1m_perfect_forwarding
r63t_ref_collapsing
dknz_value_categories

i193_std_forward.title: std::forward and Perfect Forwarding

ibwz_template_deduction.title: Template Argument Deduction
ibwz_template_deduction.content:
Context
When calling a function template without explicit template args, compiler deduces T from arguments.
Overview
Deduction compares parameter type with argument type:

























ParameterLvalue ArgRvalue ArgTT = X (decay)T = XT&T = X → param X&can’t bindT&& (forwarding ref)T = X& → param X&T = X → param X&&
Key Points

Top-level const/& are dropped for by-value T.
Arrays decay to pointers unless T& or T&&.
All Ts must deduce to same type in a single call.
Deduction failure is SFINAE in function templates.

See Also

hi6f_universal_refs — special T&& rule
yxzq_sfinae
Class Template Argument Deduction (CTAD)

k9p2_perfect_blue.title: Perfect Blue: A Masterpiece of Psychological Horror and Identity
k9p2_perfect_blue.content:
Perfect Blue: A Masterpiece of Psychological Horror and Identity
Context

Related: 7hjk_kon_cycle
Prerequisites: None — start here
Next: 7hjk_kon_cycle

Perfect Blue is a 1997 Japanese animated psychological horror film directed by Satoshi Kon, based on the novel “Perfect Blue: Complete Metamorphosis” by Yoshikazu Takeuchi. It explores themes of identity, fame, and the blurring line between reality and fantasy.
The film follows Mima Kirigoe, a Japanese pop idol who leaves her successful music group to pursue an acting career. As she transitions into darker roles, she becomes the target of a stalker and begins to lose her grip on reality. The narrative masterfully intertwines her psychological breakdown with a series of gruesome murders, questioning the nature of identity in the age of media saturation.
Plot Summary
Mima Kirigoe is a beloved member of the pop idol group CHAM!. After announcing her retirement from music to become an actress, she faces backlash from fans and receives threatening messages. She lands a role in a violent rape scene for a TV drama, which further disturbs her sense of self. As her life unravels, she experiences hallucinations and paranoia, unsure of what is real and what is imagined. The film builds to a shocking climax where the boundaries between her multiple identities collapse.
Themes and Analysis

Identity Fragmentation: Mima’s struggle to maintain her idol persona versus her “real” self reflects the performative nature of identity in the celebrity age.
Media and voyeurism: The film critiques the invasive gaze of media and fans, and how being watched can distort self-perception.
Reality vs. Fantasy: Kon’s signature fluid animation style makes it impossible to distinguish between Mima’s internal mental state and external reality.
Fame and its costs: The film examines how the pressure to conform to expectations can lead to psychological destruction.
Gaze and objectification: The film’s visual language constantly shifts perspectives, making the audience complicit in the voyeuristic violence.

Artistic Significance
Perfect Blue is widely regarded as one of the greatest anime films ever made and a landmark in psychological horror. Its influence can be seen in later works like Darren Aronofsky’s Requiem for a Dream and Black Swan (which Kon cited as an influence in return). The film’s use of surreal imagery, unreliable narration, and tight pacing set a new standard for adult-oriented animation.
Cultural Impact

Pioneered the “psychological horror” genre in anime
Demonstrated that animation could handle mature, complex themes with the depth of live-action cinema
Inspired filmmakers worldwide, not just in anime
Remains a cult classic and subject of academic study in film, psychology, and media studies

Perfect Blue is a demanding watch — it’s intense, disturbing, and emotionally exhausting. But it’s also a brilliant work of art that rewards careful analysis. The film’s power lies in its ability to make the audience feel Mima’s disorientation and terror. It’s not just a horror film; it’s a profound commentary on identity in the modern world.
For viewers new to Satoshi Kon’s work, Perfect Blue is an excellent entry point alongside Paprika and Tokyo Godfathers.

k3m8_paprika - Another Satoshi Kon film about reality/dream boundaries
Black Swan - Film influenced by Perfect Blue’s themes
Paranoia Agent - Kon’s TV series on societal anxiety
Magnetic Rose - Segment from Memories with similar psychological horror elements
Perfect Blue (manga) - The original novel adaptation

lco3_Partial-Fraction-Decomposition.title: Partial Fraction Decomposition
lco3_Partial-Fraction-Decomposition.content:
Partial Fraction Decomposition
Example
Consider
\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)}
Where
Q(x) = (x + a)(x^2 + b)(x^2 + c)^2(x + d)^3
And \text{deg}(P) < \text{deg}(Q).
Our partial fraction decomposition is:
\frac{P(x)}{Q(x)} = \frac{A_1}{x + a} + \frac{A_2 x + A_3}{x^2 + b} + \frac{A_4 x + A_5}{x^2 + c}
Remarks

Useful for integrals
Useful for solving ODEs when using uu87_Laplace Transform

oqfb_std_move.title: std::move — Unconditional Cast to Rvalue
oqfb_std_move.content:
Context
std::move is a cast, not a move operation. It unconditionally converts an expression to an xvalue (expiring value).
Signature
template<typename T>
constexpr std::remove_reference_t<T>&& move(T&& t) noexcept;
Usage
std::string s = "hello";
std::string t = std::move(s);  // s becomes valid but unspecified
The actual move happens in std::string’s move constructor, triggered because std::move(s) is an xvalue.
Pitfalls

Don’t std::move a const object — move ctor requires non-const.
Don’t std::move something you still need.
Don’t std::move temporaries (they’re already rvalues).
Don’t return std::move(local) — inhibits NRVO.

See Also

0u18_move_semantics
vf1m_perfect_forwarding
dknz_value_categories

p6m7_paprika.title: Paprika: Dreams Colliding with Reality
p6m7_paprika.content:
Paprika: Dreams Colliding with Reality
Context

Related: 7hjk_kon_cycle
Prerequisites: None — start here
Next: 7hjk_kon_cycle

Paprika is a 2006 Japanese animated science fiction psychological thriller film directed by Satoshi Kon, based on the 1993 novel by Yasutaka Tsutsui. It was Kon’s final film before his death and crystallized his signature themes of reality perception and identity.
The story follows a device called the DC Mini that allows therapists to enter patients’ dreams. When the device is stolen and dream-and-reality boundaries begin to blur, a detective and a researcher using her dream avatar “Paprika” must unravel a conspiracy that threatens to collapse the distinction between waking and sleeping worlds.
Plot Summary
Dr. Atsuko Chiba and her collaborator Dr. Toshimi Konakawa have developed the DC Mini, a device that enables psychotherapists to directly enter patients’ dreams. Atsuko uses an alter ego named “Paprika” to perform dream therapy. When several scientists begin having nightmares that spill into reality, and the device is stolen, Paprika must hunt for the culprit before the dream and real worlds merge completely.
Themes and Analysis

Reality/Perception: The film’s central question is whether any distinction between dream and reality truly matters when both are experienced as real.
Collective unconscious: The climax involves a literal merger of all characters’ subconscious minds into a giant dream entity.
Technology and the mind: The DC Mini represents the danger of externally accessing internal experience.
Identity fluidity: Characters shift forms repeatedly, challenging fixed notions of self.
Cinema as dream: The film itself is structured like a dream, with visual logic trumping narrative logic.

Artistic Significance
Paprika represents the apex of Satoshi Kon’s directorial style — fluid reality transitions, surreal imagery, and precise, terrifying pacing. The parade sequence (where a giant procession of nightmares marches through the city) is one of the most remarkable pieces of animation ever created. The film influenced Christopher Nolan’s Inception and stands as a key work demonstrating animation’s capacity for philosophical and psychological depth.
Connection to Perfect Blue
Both films deal with the fragmentation of identity and the collapse of subjective/objective boundaries. Where Perfect Blue explores identity through the lens of celebrity and media, Paprika explores it through dreams and technology. The “parade” in Paprika functions similarly to Mima’s hallucinations — an externalization of internal psychological chaos.
Paprika is a challenging film that demands multiple viewings. Unlike many dream-based narratives, it doesn’t explain away its surrealism with a “it was all a dream” twist — the film insists that dreams and reality are equally meaningful states. Kon’s death shortly after its release makes this film a poignant final statement on his lifelong obsessions.

k9p2_perfect_blue - Kon’s earlier work on identity/reality collapse
Paranoia Agent - Kon’s TV series extending these themes
Inception - Film with strikingly similar concepts
Millennium Actress - Another Kon film exploring memory and time
Dreams (film) - Kurosawa’s direct dream anthology

p8qr_compartmentalization_psychology.title: Compartmentalization (Psychology)
p8qr_compartmentalization_psychology.content:
Context
Understanding how the mind separates thoughts, emotions, and tasks to maintain mental health and efficiency. Relevant for understanding defense mechanisms, productivity, and mental disorders.
Overview
Compartmentalization is a psychological defense mechanism where a person mentally separates conflicting thoughts, feelings, or memories into separate “compartments” to avoid anxiety or cognitive dissonance. It’s like creating isolated processes in your mind that don’t interfere with each other.
Main Content
What It Is
Compartmentalization is the act of dividing your mental life into separate sections so that painful or anxiety-inducing thoughts and feelings don’t contaminate your overall wellbeing. It’s a coping strategy that can be adaptive (healthy) or maladaptive (unhealthy).
When It’s Healthy vs. Unhealthy
Healthy (adaptive):

A surgeon focusing entirely on an operation, blocking out personal worries
A parent putting work stress aside to be fully present with children
Creating work-life boundaries to prevent burnout
Emergency responders staying functional during crises by temporarily suppressing emotional responses

Unhealthy (maladaptive):

Avoiding dealing with trauma entirely, leading to PTSD symptoms
Denying serious problems (e.g., addiction, relationship issues)
Complete emotional dissociation, which can contribute to disorders like DID
When compartments become so sealed that they prevent integration and growth

Psychological Context

Part of defense mechanisms (Freudian/psychoanalytic concept)
Related to dissociation but less extreme
Can be a coping strategy for acute stress
Often seen in high-stress professions (first responders, surgeons, military)
Can become problematic when used chronically to avoid necessary emotional processing

Remarks

Compartmentalization exists on a spectrum — everyone does it to some degree.
The key difference between healthy and unhealthy is whether the compartmentalized content gets appropriate attention eventually (integration vs. avoidance).
Modern psychology sometimes critiques it as avoidance rather than processing; mindfulness practices aim to dissolve artificial boundaries.
It’s different from suppression (conscious effort to push thoughts aside) vs. repression (unconscious blocking); compartmentalization can be semi-conscious.
In PTSD, trauma memories become isolated “islands” that don’t integrate with other memories.

See Also

defense_mechanisms (if that note exists)
dissociation
cognitive_dissonance
mindfulness (opposite approach)
trauma

r63t_ref_collapsing.title: Reference Collapsing Rules
r63t_ref_collapsing.content:
Context
When a reference is formed from another reference (e.g., T& &&), C++ collapses it.
Rules

























PatternResultT& &T&T& &&T&T&& &T&T&& &&T&&
If any & appears, result is &.
Why Needed
Template deduction with T&& for lvalues yields T = X&, making parameter type X& && → collapses to X&, so lvalues bind correctly.
In Perfect Forwarding
std::forward<T>(arg) does static_cast<T&&>(arg). Collapsing ensures:

T = X& → X& && → X& (lvalue)
T = X → X&& (rvalue)

See Also

hi6f_universal_refs
vf1m_perfect_forwarding

u2my_concepts.title: Concepts (C++20)

uu87_Laplace-Transform.title: Laplace Transform
uu87_Laplace-Transform.content:
Laplace Transform
Definition
The Laplace transform of a function f(t) is defined as:
F(s) = \mathcal{L}\{f(t)\} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-st} f(t)\,dt
where s = \sigma + i\omega is a complex number.
Key Properties

Linearity: \mathcal{L}\{af(t) + bg(t)\} = aF(s) + bG(s)
Shifting: \mathcal{L}\{f(t - a)u(t - a)\} = e^{-as}F(s)
Differentiation: \mathcal{L}\{f'(t)\} = sF(s) - f(0)

Applications

Solving differential equations — transforms them into algebraic equations
In control theory for system analysis
In circuit theory for analyzing RLC networks
Converts convolution products into simple multiplication

Inverse
The inverse Laplace transform recovers f(t):
f(t) = \mathcal{L}^{-1}\{F(s)\}
Connections

Useful for ODEs, which connects to lco3_Partial Fraction Decomposition for rational transforms
Related to Fourier transform when s = i\omega

Remarks

Transforms functions from time domain to complex frequency (s-domain)

vf1m_perfect_forwarding.title: Perfect Forwarding

w0ar_Simple-Backpropagation.title: Simple Backpropagation
w0ar_Simple-Backpropagation.content:
Simple Backpropagation
Overview
Backpropagation (backward propagation of errors) is the algorithm used to train neural networks by computing gradients of the loss function with respect to each weight.
Key Idea

Forward pass: Input flows through network → output computed
Loss calculation: Compare output to expected target
Backward pass: Compute derivatives from output layer back to input layer
Gradient descent: Adjust weights in direction that minimizes loss

Chain Rule in Action
For a simple neuron:
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = \frac{\partial L}{\partial y} \cdot \frac{\partial y}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial w}
where:

L = loss
y = neuron output
z = pre-activation (w \cdot x + b)
w = weight

Simple Example (1 neuron)
Assume neuron: y = \sigma(w x + b)
Loss: L = \frac{1}{2}(y - t)^2
Then:
\frac{\partial L}{\partial w} = (y - t)\ \sigma'(w x + b)\ x
Steps in Code

Record all intermediate values during forward pass
Compute derivative of loss w.r.t. final output
Work backwards through the computational graph
Accumulate gradients for each weight parameter
Update weights: w \leftarrow w - \alpha \, \frac{\partial L}{\partial w}

Common Pitfalls

Vanishing gradients: Gradients become extremely small in deep nets (sigmoid/tanh saturation)
Exploding gradients: Gradients blow up (causes unstable training)
Local minima: Non-convex loss landscape can trap near-optimal solutions
Careful initialization: Xavier/Glorot, He init help avoid these

Applications

Training feedforward neural networks
Fine-tuning deep networks (with techniques like dropout, batch norm)
Fundamentally used by practically all deep learning systems

Related Mathematics

uu87_Laplace Transform — used in signal-processing-based neural architectures
Chain rule from calculus (multivariable differentiation)
Optimization theory (gradient descent variants)

Remarks
Backpropagation depends on:

Differentiable activation functions (\sigma, ReLU, tanh, etc.)
Clear computational graph
Smooth loss surfaces

wvgc_Systemd-Service-Section-Options.title: Systemd [Service] Section Options
wvgc_Systemd-Service-Section-Options.content:
Systemd [Service] Section — Complete Option Reference
Context

Related: 76kn_Systemd User Services
Prerequisites: Understand basic systemd unit structure
See also: man systemd.service

Overview
The [Service] section defines how a service runs: execution type, commands, environment, resource limits, security hardening, and lifecycle behavior.

Quick Reference Tables
Type & Execution








































DirectiveValuesNotesType=simple (default)Process runs directly; started when ExecStart returnsexecRun and exit immediatelyforkingDaemon forks; systemd waits for forkoneshotRun-to-completion; exit = donenotifyProgram calls sd_notify READY=1idleDelays until job queue empty
Command Directives

































DirectivePurposeExecStart=Primary start command (usually required)ExecStartPre=Pre-start setup (run sequentially, multiple allowed)ExecStartPost=Post-start actionsExecStop=Clean shutdown commandExecStopPost=Post-shutdown cleanupExecReload=Config reload (called by systemctl reload)
Specifiers: $MAINPID (main process PID), %i (instance name), %t (runtime dir), etc.
Restart Policy






























DirectiveValuesDefaultRestart=no / on-success / on-failure / on-abnormal / on-abort / alwaysnoRestartSec=Seconds100msStartLimitBurst=Integer5StartLimitIntervalSec=Seconds10s
Common: Restart=on-failure + RestartSec=5
User / Environment








































DirectiveExampleNotesUser=User=appuserDrop privilegesGroup=Group=appuserPrimary groupSupplementaryGroups=SupplementaryGroups=diskExtra groupsEnvironment=Environment="PORT=8080"Set variable (repeatable)EnvironmentFile=EnvironmentFile=/etc/default/appLoad KEY=val fileWorkingDirectory=WorkingDirectory=/var/lib/appProcess pwd
Resource Limits






























DirectiveUnitExampleLimitNOFILE=N65535MemoryLimit=B/K/M/G512MCPUQuota=%80%LimitNPROC=N4096
Security Hardening








































DirectiveValuesEffectPrivateDevices=yesHide /dev except minimalPrivateUsers=yesHide /etc/passwdProtectHome=read-only/home, /root read-onlyProtectSystem=strictMount /usr, /boot read-only (or full immutable)NoNewPrivileges=yesBlock execve gaining privilegesCapabilityBoundingSet=CAP_NET_BIND_SERVICEDrop all but listed
Typical secure baseline:
PrivateDevices=yes
PrivateUsers=yes
ProtectHome=read-only
ProtectSystem=strict
NoNewPrivileges=yes
Logging

























DirectiveValuesStandardOutput=journal (default), syslog, kmsg, ttyStandardError=SameSyslogIdentifier="mydaemon"LogLevelMax=emerg…debug
View logs: journalctl -u <service> -f
Dependencies





























DirectiveMeaningAfter=Start after these unitsBefore=Start before these unitsWants=Weak dependency; start if available, don’t fail if missingRequires=Hard dependency; fail if not activeBindsTo=Like Requires, but stop together

Complete Minimal Example
[Unit]
Description=My Background Process
After=network.target
 
[Service]
Type=simple
User=appuser
ExecStart=/usr/local/bin/myapp --config /etc/myapp.conf
Restart=on-failure
RestartSec=5
Environment="PORT=8080"
WorkingDirectory=/var/lib/myapp
SyslogIdentifier=myapp
StandardOutput=journal
 
[Install]
WantedBy=multi-user.target

Gotchas & Tips

ExecStart is required for non-oneshot services
Multiple ExecStartPre= allowed; they run sequentially
%t resolves per-context: /run (system) vs $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR (user)
Quote env with spaces: Environment="KEY=value with spaces"
Check effective config: systemctl show <service> -p <directive>
Verify syntax: systemd-analyze verify /path/to/file.service
User services: place in ~/.config/systemd/user/, manage with systemctl --user


Full Directive List (Alphabetical)
AmbientCapabilities=, CapabilityBoundingSet=, ConfigurationDirectoryMode=, DefaultDependencies=, Delegate=, DevicePolicy=, DynamicUser=, Environment=, EnvironmentFile=, ExecStart=, ExecStartPre=, ExecStartPost=, ExecStop=, ExecStopPost=, ExecReload=, Group=, IOSchedulingClass=, IOSchedulingPriority=, IPAddressDeny=, IPAddressAllow=, KeyringMode=, LockPersonality=, LogExtraFields=, LogLevelMax=, LogRateLimitBurst=, LogRateLimitIntervalSec=, MainPID=, MemoryDenyWriteExecute=, MemoryLimit=, MountAPIVFS=, NoNewPrivileges=, OOMScoreAdjust=, PAMName=, PermissionsStartOnly=, PrivateDevices=, PrivateMounts=, PrivateNetwork=, PrivateTmp=, PrivateUsers=, ProcSubset=, ProtectClock=, ProtectControlGroups=, ProtectHome=, ProtectHostname=, ProtectKernelLogs=, ProtectKernelModules=, ProtectKernelTunables=, ProtectSystem=, ReadOnlyPaths=, ReadWritePaths=, RemoveIPC=, Restart=, RestartForceExitStatus=, RestartSec=, RootDirectory=, RootImage=, RuntimeDirectory=, RuntimeDirectoryMode=, RuntimeDirectoryPreserve=, SecureBits=, SELinuxContext=, Slice=, SmackProcessLabel=, StandardError=, StandardInput=, StandardOutput=, StaticPUID=, SupplementaryGroups=, SystemCallArchitectures=, SystemCallErrorNumber=, SystemCallFilter=, SystemCallLog=, TimerSlackNSec=, TTYPath=, TTYReset=, TTYVHangup=, TTYVTDisallocate=, UMask=, User=, UtmpIdentifier=, WorkingDirectory=
(See man systemd.service for details on each.)

See Also

man systemd.service — Service unit configuration
man systemd.exec — Execution environment settings (many [Service] options live here)
man systemd.unit — Common unit directives (After, Before, Wants, etc.)
man systemd.directive — Alphabetical directive list
man systemctl — Control systemd

yxzq_sfinae.title: SFINAE — Substitution Failure Is Not An Error
yxzq_sfinae.content:
Context
If template argument substitution fails, the candidate is removed from overload resolution rather than causing a hard error.
Overview
template<typename T>
auto f(T t) -> decltype(t.size()) { return t.size(); }
 
template<typename T>
void f(T);  // fallback
 
f(42);  // first fails (no .size()), second chosen
enable_if
template<typename T,
         typename = std::enable_if_t<std::is_integral_v<T>>>
void process(T value);
Only participates if condition true.
Where SFINAE Applies

Function parameter types
Return type
Default template arguments

NOT in function body (checked later).
See Also

u2my_concepts — modern alternative
wdj9_type_traits
